title: Limits of Bidirectional Models
concept: Bidirectional RNNs require the entire sequence to be available, meaning they cannot be used for real-time streaming tasks or live text generation.

exercises.prompt: What makes Bidirectional RNNs different from standard, one-way RNNs?

options.text: They read the input sequence in both directions.
They double the size of the vocabulary.

exercises.explain: Bidirectional RNNs read sequences in both directions (forward and backward), which requires having the entire sequence available at once.

cards.title: The Time-Travel Trap
cards.body: We know Bidirectional RNNs are super smart because they look at both the past and the future. But what happens when the future hasn't happened yet? Let's look at why these models struggle with real-time tasks.

cards.title: The Limitation
cards.body: Because Bidirectional RNNs process text in both directions (forward and backward) at the same time, they require the entire sequence to be available from the start. This makes them unusable for real-time streaming where future data hasn't happened yet.

cards.title: The Delayed Translator
cards.body: Imagine trying to translate a live speech word-for-word. A bidirectional translator has to wait until the speaker finishes the entire sentence before they can start translating. They can't do live, instant translation because they are always waiting for the 'future' words to arrive.

cards.title: Live Captioning Lag
cards.body: If you are building a live captioning system for a TV broadcast, a Bidirectional RNN won't work. It needs to know the end of the sentence to understand the beginning, which means your captions would have a massive, awkward delay until the speaker stops talking.

cards.title: Is Bigger Always Better?
cards.body: People think bidirectional models are always better because they have 'more information'. In reality, they are useless for tasks like predictive texting or live control loops where you must react instantly to incoming data without knowing the future.

cards.title: Unidirectional vs. Bidirectional
cards.body: Compare how data access limits where we can use different types of RNNs.

left.text: Processes step-by-step. Perfect for real-time streaming and generating next words.

right.text: Needs the whole sequence at once. Great for analyzing static text, impossible for live streaming.

exercises.prompt: For which of these tasks is a Bidirectional RNN NOT suitable?

options.text: Analyzing the sentiment of a fully written book review.
Translating a completed document from French to English.
Predicting the next word a user is typing in real-time.
Finding named entities in a saved medical report.

exercises.explain: Bidirectional models need the complete sentence (both start and end) to run, so they cannot predict words one-by-one as they happen in a live stream.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A Bidirectional RNN is a poor choice for a live voice-assistant that needs to respond immediately to streaming audio.
exercises.explain: Bidirectional models require the entire text to be present. Since a live speaker hasn't spoken the future words yet, the model cannot process the audio in real-time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to explain the limitation of bidirectional models.
exercises.sentence: Bidirectional models cannot run in real-time because they need to read the future data.
exercises.explain: Because bidirectional models look at both the past and the future, they cannot work in real-time settings where the future data is not yet known.

exercises.prompt:
Identify the AI model type based on its behavior:
• Model A: Must wait for the user to finish typing the entire paragraph before it can start translating.
• Model B: Translates word-by-word instantly as the user types.

options.text: Model A is bidirectional, Model B is unidirectional.
Model A is unidirectional, Model B is bidirectional.

exercises.explain: Model A is bidirectional because it requires the complete sequence to be finished before processing, whereas Model B can process step-by-step as data arrives.

exercises.prompt: Why is it impossible to use a Bidirectional RNN for real-time text generation (like predicting the next word as a user types)?

options.text: They are too slow to run on modern computers.
They require the entire sequence to be finished before making predictions.
They can only process text written in English.
They completely forget the past when looking at the future.

exercises.explain: Bidirectional models are great for analyzing complete texts (like translation or sentiment analysis), but because they need the end of a sentence to calculate the beginning, they cannot generate text word-by-word in real-time or handle live audio streaming.
